subject: ap-world-history
theme: Between 1945 and the 1990s, the ideological Cold War between US-led capitalism and Soviet communism restructured global politics through proxy conflicts, nuclear deterrence, and competing models of development — while a simultaneous wave of decolonization created dozens of newly independent states that struggled to achieve genuine political and economic sovereignty beyond formal independence.

core_concepts.title: Cold War Ideology & Proxy Conflicts
core_concepts.detail: The Cold War was an ideological and geopolitical competition between the United States (capitalism, containment, NATO) and the Soviet Union (communism, Marxist-Leninist ideology, Warsaw Pact) that never resulted in direct military conflict between the two superpowers, but was fought through proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam, Angola), economic competition, and the nuclear arms race that made direct conflict suicidally irrational.

core_concepts.title: US Containment Strategy
core_concepts.detail: The United States implemented containment through the Truman Doctrine (military aid to resist communist expansion), the Marshall Plan (economic reconstruction of Western Europe), NATO (military alliance), and eventually direct military interventions — demonstrating that economic, military, and political tools all served the anti-communist strategic goal.

core_concepts.title: Decolonization & the United Nations
core_concepts.detail: Decolonization between 1945 and 1975 transformed the international system: dozens of new African and Asian states joined the United Nations, shifting its membership from European-dominated to majority-Global South. Newly independent nations pursued independence through negotiation (India, Ghana) or armed struggle (Algeria, Vietnam), and many formed the Non-Aligned Movement to resist Cold War bipolarity.

core_concepts.title: Non-Aligned Movement & Bandung
core_concepts.detail: The Non-Aligned Movement, launched at the Bandung Conference (1955) by leaders including Nehru (India), Nasser (Egypt), Sukarno (Indonesia), and Nkrumah (Ghana), argued that newly independent nations had interests distinct from both Cold War blocs and should resist superpower domination — asserting that the struggle against colonialism and for racial equality were inseparable global causes.

core_concepts.title: Neo-Colonialism & Economic Dependency
core_concepts.detail: Decolonization produced genuine political independence but often failed to deliver economic independence — newly independent states inherited colonial economic structures (raw material export, lack of industrial base, unfavorable terms of trade) designed for European benefit, creating a condition later theorized as neo-colonialism. Cold War competition intensified this pattern as superpowers competed to support client regimes.

core_concepts.title: China's Communist Revolution & Policies
core_concepts.detail: China's communist revolution (1949) and subsequent policies — Great Leap Forward (1958–62, catastrophic famine) and Cultural Revolution (1966–76, political purges) — demonstrated an alternative Marxist model that broke with Soviet orthodoxy, eventually producing the Sino-Soviet Split and establishing China as an independent pole in the communist world.

core_concepts.title: Soviet Collapse & Cold War End
core_concepts.detail: The Cold War ended as Gorbachev's reforms (glasnost and perestroika) unleashed forces that destabilized the Soviet system — Eastern European communist regimes fell between 1989 and 1991, the Berlin Wall was torn down, German reunification occurred, and the Soviet Union dissolved into 15 independent republics, transforming the global balance of power.

exam_tip: Unit 8 emphasizes causation and CCOT — the AP exam frequently asks about the causes and effects of Cold War competition, how decolonization varied in different regions (negotiated vs. armed, Asia vs. Africa vs. Middle East), and how Cold War dynamics shaped newly independent states' development. Pay close attention to the connections between Cold War competition and proxy wars, and the relationships between figures like Gandhi (nonviolent resistance), Mandela (anti-apartheid), Nasser (pan-Arab nationalism), and Ho Chi Minh (communist independence movement) — the AP exam uses these figures as lenses for broader historical patterns. The relationship between formal independence and continued economic dependency is a frequent source of essay prompts.
